commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=36dfb369e7afc5682fcde9b12eec4eed1b69b90c)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds direct Azure Windows OS-disk checkpoint creation, snapshot-fork VM provisioning, credential rehydration, and verify/delete handling for direct checkpoint records.

Reproducibility: yes. for the review finding: source inspection shows `checkpoint create` defaults `--no-reboot` to true, while the new Azure driver reaches a `BeginDeallocate` call without checking `req.NoReboot`. No live Azure run was performed in this review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The PR needs a code fix for the no-reboot contract and contributor-side standalone live proof before maintainers can safely continue normal review.

target.title: feat(azure): fork windows leases from snapshots
target.body:
## Why

Azure Windows desktop leases take several minutes to provision and then require machine-level setup before application tests can begin. Crabbox needs a native checkpoint path that can snapshot a prepared Windows OS disk and fork new disposable leases from it without reusing lease credentials or relying on a marketplace image boot.

The restored VM must receive fresh SSH, Windows, and VNC credentials, and the extension command must stay within Azure's command-size constraints.

## What Changed

- Advertise direct Azure Windows disk snapshots as a native checkpoint capability.
- Create checkpoints by deallocating the source VM and snapshotting its managed OS disk.
- Fork a checkpoint by creating a managed disk from the snapshot and attaching it to a specialized Windows VM without an image reference or OS profile.
- Set the attached OS disk type explicitly to Windows.
- Rehydrate fresh SSH keys, SSH host keys, Windows credentials, auto-logon state, and TightVNC credentials on every fork.
- Compress the rehydration PowerShell and execute it inline so realistic ed25519 keys remain below Azure's extension command-size limit.
- Support inspect, verify, delete, and Windows-specific workdir behavior for direct Azure checkpoint records.
- Reject unsupported Azure Windows checkpoint strategies instead of silently selecting an incompatible image workflow.

## How to Test

1. Create and prepare an Azure Windows desktop lease.
2. Run:

   ```console
   crabbox checkpoint create \
     --provider azure \
     --target windows \
     --windows-mode normal \
     --id cbx_<lease> \
     --strategy disk-snapshot \
     --wait
   ```

3. Fork the resulting checkpoint:

   ```console
   crabbox checkpoint fork chk_<checkpoint> \
     --provider azure \
     --target windows \
     --windows-mode normal \
     --desktop \
     --type Standard_D4s_v5
   ```

4. Confirm the fork has a new lease ID, SSH host key, Windows password, and VNC password, and that SSH and VNC become ready.
5. Verify and delete the checkpoint through the checkpoint CLI.
6. As a regression check, request an unsupported Azure Windows checkpoint strategy and confirm Crabbox rejects it.

Live validation:

- Created and verified Azure checkpoint `chk_16fcf21c8c70bd5c`.
- Forked multiple `Standard_D4s_v5` Windows leases from the checkpoint.
- Completed Sqatch Codex App smoke run `f8fdd67b-3007-426b-8565-c2475ccfc101` successfully from a snapshot fork.
- Live validation combined this branch locally with the deterministic TightVNC startup work in [#689](https://github.com/openclaw/crabbox/pull/689).

Targeted tests:

- `go test ./internal/providers/azure ./internal/cli`

## Related

- [#689](https://github.com/openclaw/crabbox/pull/689) provides the deterministic TightVNC startup used by the local integration build.
target.author: fcoury-oai
